followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the weak-evidence verdict and similar overall score, but I revise the objection set. The other reviews persuaded me that the probe-scalar-versus-DBI-hair distinction is a real missing assumption. I also soften my earlier scalar-table 'no peak found for l=1/l=2 at small regularity' objection because the HTML bundle loses table labels and numerical entries; it remains an important human spot-check rather than a confident paper flaw.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: The claim that Pade-WKB self-consistency 'firmly establishes' numerical reliability is too strong; agreement between related asymptotic approximants is not an independent benchmark.
The very high technical-soundness score in one review is not justified given the limited time-domain validation, unclear branch range, and missing reproducibility details.

Treating reliance on an arXiv background solution as a standalone objection is weak unless a concrete error in that background is identified; it is better framed as a dependency requiring spot-checking.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The probe scalar should be explicitly distinguished from the background DBI scalar; otherwise readers may mistake a test Klein-Gordon calculation for the coupled scalar-hair perturbation sector.
The phantom branch/background stability issue is relevant to physical interpretation, though it does not by itself invalidate fixed-background test-field QNMs.
The provided HTML rendering makes the numerical tables hard or impossible to audit, so quantitative claims should be checked against the PDF or source.
The grey-body-factor discussion is largely prospective and should not be counted as an additional computed result.

main_claims.claim: Massless scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac test fields reduce to Schrodinger-type master equations with spin-dependent effective potentials that vanish at the event horizon and spatial infinity.
main_claims.notes: The reductions are standard for static spherical backgrounds and appear plausible, but the rendered formulae obscure some metric-specific details. This supports test-field dynamics, not full coupled perturbations of the Einstein-DBI system.

main_claims.claim: Increasing the DBI regularity parameter lowers and broadens the effective-potential barriers in all three displayed spin sectors.
main_claims.notes: The representative plots and qualitative discussion support this over the sampled cases, but the paper does not give an analytic argument for the full black-hole parameter range.

main_claims.claim: For the displayed fundamental modes, increasing the regularity parameter decreases both the oscillation frequency and the damping rate.
main_claims.notes: The trend is stated repeatedly and is physically consistent with lowered barriers. However, the provided HTML bundle renders the table entries and some labels poorly, so the quantitative core is not fully auditable from this source alone.
main_claims.paper_location: Sec. VI, Tables 1-3 and discussion after Table 4

main_claims.claim: The spectral shifts are robust because they exceed the estimated numerical uncertainty from WKB16/Pade versus WKB14/Pade comparisons and limited time-domain Prony checks.
main_claims.notes: Agreement between nearby high-order Pade-WKB approximants is useful but can miss common systematic error, especially for low multipoles and overtones. Time-domain checks cover only scalar and electromagnetic examples, not the full parameter set or Dirac sector.

main_claims.claim: The two Dirac partner potentials are isospectral, so analyzing one partner potential is sufficient.
main_claims.notes: This is standard for supersymmetric partner potentials under compatible boundary conditions, but the paper does not explicitly discuss possible exceptional modes or boundary-domain subtleties.

main_claims.claim: QNM data could be used to approximate grey-body factors through the cited QNM/grey-body-factor correspondence.
main_claims.notes: The discussion is appropriately framed as a possible use of the computed spectra rather than as a completed grey-body-factor calculation.
main_claims.paper_location: Sec. VI, Eq. (26) and surrounding discussion

missing_assumptions.assumption: The massless Klein-Gordon scalar is an independent probe field, not the perturbation of the phantom DBI scalar that sources the background.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this assumption, the scalar sector of the underlying theory should generally be a coupled metric-plus-DBI-scalar perturbation problem, so the reported scalar QNMs would not represent the physical hair perturbations.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Explicit parameter range for the black-hole branch and for all sampled regularity values.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. II and Sec. VI tables
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The boundary conditions, single-exterior tortoise coordinate, and WKB setup depend on having a nonextremal event horizon rather than an extremal remnant or horizonless regular object.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Precise definition of the relative-difference/error measure used in the tables.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Tables 1-4 captions and Sec. VI discussion
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper compares complex frequencies from different WKB-Pade orders, but the rendered text does not specify whether the percentage difference uses the complex modulus, the real part, the imaginary part, or another convention.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Independent convergence criteria for peak finding and WKB derivative evaluation.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. V WKB method and Sec. VI tables
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Peak finding is central to WKB applicability. Any 'no peak found' entries or near-flat-barrier cases require reproducible domain choices and numerical tolerances.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Classical stability of the phantom-DBI-supported background, or a clear statement that only fixed-background probe-field spectra are being computed.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the underlying phantom matter sector or coupled gravitational-scalar perturbations are unstable, the test-field spectra may still be mathematically well defined but have weaker physical ringdown significance.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What are the actual numerical entries and labels in Tables 1-4 in the PDF or TeX source, and do they reproduce the claimed trends and Schwarzschild-limit behavior?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The supplied arXiv HTML text drops or corrupts many table values and mathematical labels, so the quantitative claims cannot be fully checked from the bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do any scalar table entries marked 'no peak found' correspond to multipoles or regularity values for which the exterior scalar potential should plainly have a Schwarzschild-like barrier?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: My prior objection depended on interpreting missing table labels. A human should reproduce the scalar potential and peak search from the exact metric and table parameters.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the full Einstein-phantom-DBI system have a stable black-hole branch over the sampled regularity range, and how do its coupled gravitational/scalar perturbations behave?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires analyzing the underlying theory and likely the separate Parvez-Shankaranarayanan construction, which is outside the supplied paper text.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the WKB16/WKB14 Pade frequencies benchmarked against direct integration, continued fractions, or time-domain extraction for the low-multipole scalar and Dirac cases where WKB systematics are most likely?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper gives only limited time-domain examples and does not provide enough numerical code or settings to reproduce a broader benchmark from the bundle alone.

novelty_assessment: The novelty remains modest. The paper applies standard test-field perturbation equations, high-order Pade-WKB estimates, and a small number of time-domain checks to a newly proposed DBI-supported regular black-hole geometry. The specific background and spin-by-spin catalogue have incremental value, but the methods and qualitative conclusions are close to a large existing literature on regular-black-hole QNMs.
score_rationale: The paper is a plausible and useful routine computation, but its strongest claims rest on limited and not fully reproducible numerical validation. The probe-field setup is standard once stated clearly, yet the scalar channel, phantom background stability, black-hole branch range, and WKB reliability need tighter qualification. The result is worth attention as an incremental catalogue, not as a technically decisive or field-shaping contribution.
summary: The paper is an incremental QNM study of massless test scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac fields on a recently proposed phantom-DBI-supported regular black-hole metric. The standard master-equation setup is plausible, and the qualitative trend that increasing the regularity scale lowers the potential barriers and shifts representative QNMs toward smaller real and imaginary parts is credible. After reading the other reviews, I retain the main concern that the numerical robustness claims are stronger than the evidence, and I now add a sharper conceptual caveat: the scalar channel is a probe Klein-Gordon field and should be explicitly distinguished from perturbations of the DBI hair. I also downgrade my earlier scalar 'no peak found' objection from a firm major objection to a human-spotcheck item, because the HTML-rendered tables lose crucial labels and numbers.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper treats the difference between two nearby high-order Pade-WKB approximants as the numerical uncertainty and concludes that the shifts are genuine. Such approximants can agree while sharing systematic bias, particularly for low multipoles and higher overtones. Only two time-domain checks are shown, apparently for scalar and electromagnetic fundamental modes, with no Dirac check and no systematic sweep over the delicate cases.
technical_objections.objection: The numerical uncertainty estimate is too weak for the paper's strongest robustness claims.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. VI, paragraphs comparing WKB16/Pade and WKB14/Pade; Abstract robustness claim

technical_objections.evidence: For a hairy black-hole solution, the physical scalar-led perturbation sector of the underlying Einstein-DBI theory would generally involve perturbations of the background scalar and metric, not just a minimally coupled probe Klein-Gordon equation. The paper can legitimately study an independent test scalar, but it should state this assumption clearly and avoid implying it is the DBI-hair perturbation channel.
technical_objections.objection: The scalar perturbation is introduced as a free Klein-Gordon test field without explicitly distinguishing it from perturbations of the background phantom DBI scalar hair.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.1, Eq. (7); Sec. II phantom DBI background

technical_objections.evidence: The QNM boundary conditions and the tortoise-coordinate discussion require an event horizon and a standard exterior scattering problem. The paper says it works in the black-hole regime, but the rendered text does not give the critical regularity value or explicitly verify that all tabulated parameter values remain away from the extremal/remnant or horizonless configurations of the same solution.
technical_objections.objection: The black-hole branch and extremal/horizonless boundary of the sampled regularity parameter range are not stated clearly enough.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. II and start of Sec. VI

technical_objections.evidence: The methods section cites good WKB concordance when the angular number is at least the overtone index, while Table 4 includes electromagnetic overtones up to n=3 for a fixed low multipole. This does not undermine the fundamental-mode results, but it weakens claims about higher-overtone reliability based mainly on WKB-Pade self-consistency.
technical_objections.objection: The higher-overtone electromagnetic WKB calculation includes a case outside the reliability condition cited by the paper itself.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. V WKB discussion and Sec. VI Table 4

technical_objections.evidence: The paper does not provide grid spacing, initial pulse parameters, extraction location, fitting window, Prony order, convergence checks, or sensitivity to the ringdown interval. These details matter because the time-domain fits are used as independent support for WKB frequencies.
technical_objections.objection: The time-domain validation is not specified in enough detail to be independently audited.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. V time-domain integration; Sec. VI Figs. 3-4

technical_objections.evidence: The computations cover selected spins, multipoles, overtones, and regularity parameters, with limited independent validation. Statements that the DBI regularity imprint is robust should be restricted to the sampled test-field modes and to the black-hole branch where the single-barrier WKB assumptions have been checked.
technical_objections.objection: Several conclusions are phrased more globally than the evidence warrants.
